request.question: Who was the 2nd year head coach of the team who played against Florida State in the 101st Rose Bowl?

title: 1976 Rose Bowl
1995 Rose Bowl
1975 Rose Bowl
1965 Rose Bowl
2013 Michigan State Spartans football team
1954 UCLA Bruins football team
J.K. Kennedy
502nd Infantry Regiment (United States)
2014 Oregon Ducks football team
2015 Rose Bowl
sentences: The 1976 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 1976.
It was the 62nd Rose Bowl Game.
The UCLA Bruins defeated the number one ranked and undefeated Ohio State Buckeyes 23–10 in a rare bowl rematch of a regular season game.
UCLA quarterback John Sciarra was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.

This was the first post season that football teams from the Big Ten Conference and Pacific-10 Conference could appear in other bowl games since the Rose Bowl agreement was signed for the 1947 Rose Bowl.
This game marked Ohio State coach Woody Hayes' last appearance in the Rose Bowl game.
The 1995 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on Monday January 2, 1995, because New Year's Day was on a Sunday.
It was the 81st Rose Bowl Game.
The Penn State Nittany Lions defeated the Oregon Ducks 38–20.
Ki-Jana Carter of Penn State and Danny O'Neil of Oregon both were named the Rose Bowl Players of the Game.
Many observers point to this game as the one that made Ki-Jana Carter a number one selection in the 1995 NFL Draft.
The 1975 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 1975.
It was the 61st Rose Bowl Game.
Fifth ranked USC Trojans defeated third ranked Ohio State Buckeyes 18-17 in one of the most exciting games in the history of the Rose Bowl.
USC quarterback Pat Haden passed to Shelton Diggs for the two-point conversion that gave the Trojans the Rose Bowl victory and the UPI's college football national title.
The 1965 Rose Bowl, played on January 1, 1965, was the 51st Rose Bowl Game.
The Michigan Wolverines defeated the Oregon State Beavers by a score of 34–7.
Michigan fullback Mel Anthony was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.
The 2013 Michigan State Spartans football team represented Michigan State University in the Legends Division of the Big Ten Conference during the 2013 NCAA Division I FBS football season.
Michigan State played their home games at Spartan Stadium in East Lansing, Michigan and were led by seventh year head coach Mark Dantonio.
The Spartans finished the year 13–1, 8–0 and undefeated in Big Ten play.
By winning the Legends Division, they earned a trip to the Big Ten Championship Game, their second appearance in the championship game.
Facing No. 2-ranked Ohio State, they defeated the Buckeyes 34–24, knocking Ohio State out of the running for the BCS National Championship Game.
The Spartans received an invitation to the Rose Bowl, their first trip to the Rose Bowl since 1988.
There they defeated No. 5-ranked Stanford for the school's first Rose Bowl win since 1988.
The 1954 UCLA Bruins football team represented the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) in the 1954 college football season.
They played their home games at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum and were coached by Henry Russell Sanders.
It was Sanders' sixth season as the UCLA head coach.
The Bruins finished 9–0 overall, and were Pacific Coast Conference Champions with a 6–0 record.
The Bruins compiled 367 points against their opponents in nine games, and allowed only 40 points.
The Bruins were not eligible to play in the Rose Bowl vs. Ohio State because of the "no repeat" rule in effect at the time.
No team was allowed to participate in consecutive Rose Bowl games, and since UCLA had played in the 1954 Rose Bowl, they were excluded from the 1955 event.
USC competed in the Rose Bowl instead, losing to Ohio State.
Following the outcome, UCLA and Ohio State split the national championship since UCLA was ranked #1 by the UPI Coaches Poll and Ohio State was ranked #1 by the AP Poll.
Jesse Kennard "Bud" Kennedy was Florida State men's basketball head coach from 1949 to 1966.
He was born on April 22, 1907 in Lawrence, Kansas.
His father, A. R. Kennedy, was head coach of the Kansas football team.
Kennedy was an athlete at University of Kansas, Baker University, and Kansas State Teachers College.
In 1948 he became the Florida State Seminoles Athletic Manager and Assistant head coach.
The following year, he became the head coach.
Kennedy went 234–208 in his 18 years at Florida State and won the Florida Intercollegiate Conference Championship in 1955.
In 1966, on June, 26 he died of cancer.
Kennedy was 59.
Later in 1977 he was inducted into the Florida State University Hall of Fame.
The 502nd Infantry Regiment (502nd INF), previously titled the 502nd Parachute Infantry Regiment (502nd PIR), is an airborne infantry regiment of the United States Army.

The regiment was established shortly after the American entry into World War II, and was assigned as a regiment of the 101st Airborne Division, "The Screaming Eagles", one of the most decorated formations of the U.S. Army.
The regiment saw substantial action in the European Theater of World War II and was deactivated in 1945, shortly after the end of the war.

Reactivating in a new form in 1956, the 502nd Infantry has served in the Vietnam War, the first Gulf War, Operation Iraqi Freedom, Operation Enduring Freedom in Afghanistan, and Operation Inherent Resolve in Iraq.
It was removed from parachute status with the rest of the 101st Airborne Division in 1969, and since 1974, the regiment has been classified as an Air Assault unit.
Currently, its 1st and 2nd battalions are active.
Both battalions are assigned to the 2nd Brigade Combat Team, 101st Airborne Division (Air Assault).
The 2014 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2014 NCAA Division I FBS football season.
The team was led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 48th straight year.
They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

The 2015 Rose Bowl (officially known as the College Football Playoff Semifinal at the Rose Bowl Game presented by Northwestern Mutual) was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 2015, at the Rose Bowl stadium in Pasadena, California.

This 101st Rose Bowl Game, as a semifinal for the College Football Playoff (CFP), matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles as selected by the system's selection committee to compete for a spot at the National Championship game to be played on January 12, 2015 at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.
It was one of the 2014–15 bowl games that concluded the 2014 FBS football season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here. Emit exactly one field named `summary`.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A one-line header naming the primary topic(s) identified in the passages.
- 1–4 short lines enumerating concrete facts directly supported by the passages. Extract entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Do not add or infer facts not present in the passages.
- An explicit statement that names the missing bridge fact or relation needed for the next hop (express in abstract role terms, e.g., "missing bridge fact: [relation] between [candidate entity] and [target attribute]"). If passages supply candidate bridge entities but do not confirm the required relation, label them as "possible bridge entity" and state what is unverified for each.
- 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (highest priority first). Each directive must be concrete and section-aware: specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Early life", "History"), or tight search queries/keywords. Prefer section-level targets and tight searches over broad suggestions.

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` compact: one-line header plus a total of 1–4 brief lines (sentences or bullet lines) covering the factual spans, the missing bridge fact, and the prioritized retrieval directives.
- Preserve exact factual spans from the passages; when quoting, keep quotes very short (<= ~15 words) and only if verbatim in the passages.
- Do not assert a final answer or resolve facts here. If the passages do fully and unambiguously contain the required relation, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but still do not produce a final answer.
- If multiple candidate entities or variant names appear, enumerate the top 1–2 candidates and mark the name as ambiguous, stating what is unverified for each.
- Do not invent facts, hypothesize, or add extraneous context not grounded in the provided passages.

Output expectation: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary as specified above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Additional preference for multi-anchor ambiguity and parenthetical disambiguators:
- When the first-hop state signals ambiguity between two or more next-hop anchors or shows explicit parenthetical disambiguators, prefer a compact connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens in the order shown in `summary_1`, preserving any parenthetical disambiguators exactly, and append at most one short attribute or type token naming the needed relation. Keep the query a single-line retrieval string and remain token-limited.

Priority rules for ambiguity and disambiguation:
- If summary_1 lists multiple candidate anchors or otherwise signals ambiguity, emit a single connector-style query that concatenates the prioritized anchor tokens (use the order or priority visible in summary_1) and then append exactly one short attribute token or a visible year token for disambiguation.
- If summary_1 contains a comma-separated list of individuals and the missing evidence concerns a member, prefer the earliest-listed individual token paired with the visible group token and append the single attribute token member.
- If the question contains a visible year token that disambiguates a title, include that year token before the attribute token in the query.
- If none of the above applies, emit a single-anchor query using the most relevant visible token and at most one short attribute token when the question implies an attribute.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (prefer the form used in the context when there is a name-variant conflict across inputs) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Follow these additional concise operational rules when integrating evidence:
- Prefer the context-preferred name form for the preserved first (answer) sentence; when choosing a canonical name for supporting sentences, prefer the context form if there is a name-variant conflict across inputs.
- If a passage explicitly contradicts a preserved context sentence, replace the preserved sentence with a passage-supported first sentence or note the contradiction concisely in that first sentence.
- For each supporting sentence, start with the chosen canonical name and include only attributes explicitly present in the provided passages; if the passages do not assert a requested status or attribute, state that absence briefly rather than inferring the attribute.
- Do not add fields, requests for more retrieval, or change the input/output interface. Keep the `summary` to 1–3 sentences exactly and avoid extra commentary or formatting.
- Do not emit diagnostic meta-comments or a 'missing-evidence' note as the output. If the `context` contains a diagnostic or missing-evidence message instead of a concise answer sentence, replace that diagnostic text with a single answer-first sentence grounded in the provided `context` and `passages` (using context-preferred names when present).
- Allow an attested name variant in parentheses immediately after a name only if that variant appears in the provided passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add any new facts or external information.
- First run a mandatory binary detection: determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to extraction.

Conservative extractive tie-breaking (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- When a contiguous longer verbatim phrase in either summary strictly contains a shorter candidate (adds adjacent words that are part of the name or label), emit the longest contiguous verbatim phrase exactly as it appears in the summaries.
- For personal names where one verbatim form is a substring of another, prefer the longest verbatim personal-name span found in either summary; if multiple distinct spans tie for length, prefer the form from `summary_2`.
- If either summary presents an alternate token parenthetically immediately adjacent to a name/place (e.g., canonical short form in parentheses), emit that parenthetical token exactly as shown. If both summaries present parenthetical tokens, prefer the one in `summary_2`.
- If the summaries do not explicitly state the requested relation but exactly one candidate entity of the relevant role appears verbatim across the summaries and no summary contradicts the attribution, emit that single verbatim entity. If multiple distinct candidates appear or there is any contradiction, do not apply this rule and return the unresolved outcome.
- Prefer wording from `summary_2` only as a tie-breaker, except when the longest-contiguous-span or parenthetical-token rules require choosing a longer or parenthetical form present in `summary_1`.

Additional targeted extraction refinements (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer and when an explicit contiguous answer-bearing span is present in the summaries):
- Year extraction: If the question asks for a year and `summary_2` contains an explicit contiguous four-digit year token directly tied in language to the requested relation (for example, phrased so the year is attached to the award/event in question), emit that four-digit token verbatim as the entire `answer`, unless `summary_1` explicitly contradicts that year. If `summary_1` contradicts the year, do not emit the year and fall back to unresolved or other extraction rules.
- Age extraction: When the chosen extractive span exactly matches the contiguous pattern "<digits> years old" in either summary, emit only the digits (the minimal numeric token) as the final `answer`. Do not alter other age phrasings.
- City from comma-delimited place strings: If the question requests a city and the best-supported verbatim place string in the summaries is a comma-delimited span "<A>, <B>", emit only the substring before the first comma (<A>) as the minimal city answer, provided that pre-comma token is unambiguous across the summaries; if ambiguous, fall back to unresolved behavior.
- Ordinal numeric preference: For numeric/count questions, if a verbatim ordinal-form token (e.g., "1st", "2nd", "3rd" with suffix) appears exactly in the summaries as the answer, prefer and emit that ordinal-form token exactly (including suffix) rather than normalizing to digits-only.
- Comparative release-date selection: For comparative "which X was earlier" questions, if exactly one candidate has an explicit release-date/year token verbatim in the summaries and the other candidate has no release-date mention, select and emit the candidate that has the explicit dated evidence as the earlier item. Emit only the minimal verbatim candidate name. If both candidates are dated or both undated, follow existing tie-breaking or unresolved behavior.

Notes on these refinements:
- Apply these refinements only when the summaries themselves contain the explicit contiguous token or pattern described; do not invent or infer missing tokens.
- Do not require corroboration from both summaries when `summary_2` contains an explicit contiguous answer-bearing token unless `summary_1` explicitly contradicts it.
- Do not add words, punctuation, units, or explanation to the extracted minimal token.
- Do not normalize or reformat verbatim tokens except where the targeted trimming rules specify (digits-only for exact "<digits> years old" patterns or trimming comma-delimited places to the pre-comma city).

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest contiguous verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind"). If multiple adjacent phrases appear, prefer the longest contiguous phrase unless that creates contradiction.
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

Use only the provided summaries as evidence and follow the conservative tie-breaking rules above when extracting the final minimal verbatim span.

response.hop1_query: Who was the 2nd year head coach of the team who played against Florida State in the 101st Rose Bowl?
hop1_docs: 1957 Stanford Indians football team | The 1957 Stanford Indians football team represented Stanford University in the 1957 college football season.  The team was coached by Chuck Taylor in his seventh year as head coach.  Taylor was a popular coach who had led the Indians to the 1952 Rose Bowl and who had been an All-American player for the Indians who starred on the undefeated 1940 "Wow Boys" team that won the 1941 Rose Bowl and the national championship in several polls.  Just before the team's final game against archrival California, Taylor announced he was retiring as head coach to become assistant athletic director of the university.

1999 Michigan State Spartans football team | The 1999 Michigan State Spartans football team represented Michigan State University in the 1999 NCAA Division I-A football season season.  The Spartans played their home games at Spartan Stadium in East Lansing, Michigan.  This was the last year for head coach Nick Saban, who left the program on December 5 to take the head coaching position at Louisiana State.  During the bowl game, the Spartans were coached by interim head coach Bobby Williams, who led the Spartans to a 37–34 victory in the 2000 Florida Citrus Bowl over the Florida Gators of the Southeastern Conference, with a last second, game-winning field goal by kicker Paul Edinger.

2015 Rose Bowl | The 2015 Rose Bowl (officially known as the College Football Playoff Semifinal at the Rose Bowl Game presented by Northwestern Mutual) was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 2015, at the Rose Bowl stadium in Pasadena, California.  This 101st Rose Bowl Game, as a semifinal for the College Football Playoff (CFP), matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles as selected by the system's selection committee to compete for a spot at the National Championship game to be played on January 12, 2015 at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.  It was one of the 2014–15 bowl games that concluded the 2014 FBS football season.

2016 South Florida Bulls football team | The 2016 South Florida Bulls football team represented the University of South Florida (USF) in the 2016 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The 2016 season is the 20th season for the Bulls, and their fourth as a member of the American Athletic Conference (The American).  They played their home games at Raymond James Stadium in Tampa, Florida, and were led during the regular season by head coach Willie Taggart, who was in his fourth year.  Following the regular season, but before the team's appearance in the Birmingham Bowl, Taggart left to take the head coaching vacancy at Oregon, with co-offensive coordinator T. J. Weist named as interim head coach for the bowl game.

1986 Indiana Hoosiers football team | The 1986 Indiana Hoosiers football team represented Indiana University during the 1986 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They participated as members of the Big Ten Conference.  The Hoosiers played their home games at Memorial Stadium in Bloomington, Indiana.  The team was led by Bill Mallory, who was serving in his third year as head coach of the Hoosiers.  The Hoosiers participated in the All-American Bowl against Florida State, losing 13–27.

1984 Rose Bowl | The 1984 Rose Bowl game, played on January 2, was the 70th Rose Bowl Game.  The UCLA Bruins defeated the Illinois Fighting Illini by a score of 45–9.  Rick Neuheisel, UCLA quarterback, was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.  He completed 22 of 32 passes for 298 yards and four touchdowns. Neuheisel, who was named head coach of Bruins in December 2007, threw two touchdown passes to his eventual predecessor as Bruin head coach, Karl Dorrell.  The game was played on January 2, as New Year's Day fell on a Sunday.

1996 Ohio State Buckeyes football team | The 1996 Ohio State Buckeyes football team represented the Ohio State University in the 1996 NCAA Division I-A football season.  The team's head coach was John Cooper.  The Buckeyes played their home games in Ohio Stadium.  The team finished the season with a win-loss record of 11–1, and a Big Ten Conference record of 7–1.  They were co-champions of Big Ten Conference with the Northwestern Wildcats and played in the 1997 Rose Bowl against Pacific-10 Confernece champion the Arizona State Sun Devils.  Ohio State did not play Northwestern during the regular season and were selected to play in the Rose Bowl due to their better overall record of 10–1 (the Wildcats were 9–2).  Northwestern had played in the previous Rose Bowl.

hop2_docs: 2014 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2014 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2014 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 48th straight year.  They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

List of Oregon Ducks head football coaches | The Oregon Ducks football program is a college football team representing the University of Oregon that is a member of the Pac-12 Conference.  The team has had 32 head coaches since its founding in 1894.  The Ducks have played in more than 1,100 games in 113 seasons.  In those seasons, eight different coaches have led Oregon to bowl games: Hugo Bezdek, Shy Huntington, Jim Aiken, Len Casanova, Rich Brooks, Mike Bellotti, Chip Kelly and Mark Helfrich.  Conference championships have been won by Huntington, Prink Callison, Jim Aiken, Casanova, Brooks, Bellotti, Kelly, and Mark Helfrich.  Brooks is the all-time leader in games coached; Mike Bellotti holds the record for most victories, while Chip Kelly is the leader in win percentage for coaches with more than one season of service.

2013 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2013 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2013 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by first year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 47th consecutive year.  They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

2016 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2016 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2016 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by fourth-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 50th straight year.  They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

2015 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2015 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2015 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by third-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 49th straight year.  They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

Mark Helfrich (American football) | Mark August Helfrich (born October 28, 1973) is a former American football coach and is currently a college football analyst for Fox Sports.  He was the head coach for the University of Oregon from 2013 to 2016.  He was fired after a 4–8 finish to the 2016 season.

2009 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2009 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2009 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by head coach Chip Kelly in his first season as a head coach at the Division I FBS level.  Kelly was only the third Ducks head coach since 1977 and led the Ducks to a Pac-10 Championship and was named Pac-10 Coach of the Year.  He took over for Mike Bellotti.
